resources.string: Continuous monitor is waiting for an active RIPDPI session.
Find session, path mode, or verdict
Filter by source, severity, or message
Find mode, network, state, or failure
Start RIPDPI at least once to capture durable runtime sessions, samples, and failures.
Run a raw-path or in-path scan to persist reports and probe details here.
Runtime failures and native events will appear here once RIPDPI records them.
Persistent history moved out of Diagnostics
Use the dedicated History screen for saved connection sessions, previous scans, and the durable event feed.
Build a concise incident brief from the latest or selected diagnostics session and send it through the Android share sheet.
Build a ZIP with the focused session, recent live telemetry, and global runtime events, then send it directly through the Android share sheet.
Save %1$s locally. The archive keeps raw machine-readable report data but redacts network identity fields in summary and manifest files.
Create the complete archive for %1$s with structured report JSON, summary text, and telemetry CSV.
Store the current Android logcat snapshot alongside diagnostics exports when you need a raw local trace.
Start RIPDPI when you want to route traffic through this device.
Grant Android VPN permission if prompted. Setup continues on-device.
Traffic is currently routed through the selected local mode.
Review the message below and try again.
%1$s Use the connection button to continue the permission flow.
%1$s Open Settings or use the button below to repair access.
Open Diagnostics and review approach analytics
History keeps durable connection sessions, sampled runtime diagnostics, and native failures outside the live Diagnostics tools.
Balanced defaults for most networks. Keeps the local VPN path and safe RIPDPI tuning.
Use the local proxy path with the same baseline tuning when VPN mode is not desired.
Manual tuning for DNS, proxy endpoint, engine values, and optional command line overrides.
Ignored while proxy mode is active
Editing %1$s. Changes validate immediately and are stored on this device.
Used for the local VPN route.
DNS override is only applied in local VPN mode.
Where the local proxy listener binds.
Accepted range follows the current service port validation.
Upper bound for concurrent local sessions.
Strategy passed through to the RIPDPI engine.
Leave empty to use the engine default.
Use [tcp] and [udp] sections. Markers accept numeric or semantic offsets. The Hostfake profile below explains hostname-targeted fake chunks and shows ready-to-paste HTTP and TLS examples.
Accepts offsets like 1, host, method+2, midsld-1, or 1+s.
Accepts numeric or semantic offsets like 0, host+1, endhost-2, or 1+se.
0 = input-sized, positive = exact target bytes, negative = input minus N.
Accepts offsets like 0, host, sniext, extlen, or 1+s.
TLS record steps now live inside the strategy chain editor as tlsrec markers.
UDP fake bursts now live inside the strategy chain editor as fake_burst counts.
Remember the winning TCP route per HTTP Host or TLS SNI and prefer it on future connections.
How long a failing host route stays deprioritized before RIPDPI can prefer it again.
Maximum number of remembered hosts stored on this device.
TCP HTTP/TLS only. Learned hosts are remembered across restarts.

Visual host learning settings are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --host-autolearn, --host-autolearn-penalty-ttl, --host-autolearn-max-hosts, and optional --host-autolearn-file in command line settings.
This session is learning hosts through command line flags. Edit raw RIPDPI arguments to change the next start.
RIPDPI is currently remembering winning TCP routes for HTTP Host and TLS SNI traffic on this device.
Host learning is enabled in settings, but the current RIPDPI session started before this change.
The running RIPDPI session is still using host learning from an earlier start.
Host learning will begin the next time RIPDPI starts.
A learned-host store is already present on this device. Clear it if you want RIPDPI to rebuild host preferences from scratch.
RIPDPI will use normal route selection without remembering hosts.
Stored host routes are available on this device.
Forget is unavailable here because command line settings can point RIPDPI at a different learned-host file.
Nothing is stored yet. RIPDPI writes the learned-host file after it successfully learns a host.
No stored host routes are available to clear yet.
Clearing the stored file does not change the active session until RIPDPI restarts.
Use this if you want the next RIPDPI start to relearn every host from scratch.
Control whether RIPDPI decrypts QUIC Initial packets to recover SNI for UDP host routing, filters, cache records, and telemetry.
Disabled skips QUIC inspection. Route by SNI uses the recovered hostname for live routing only. Route and cache also stores QUIC-derived hosts for repeated UDP flows.
Allow hostname extraction from QUIC version 1 Initial packets.
Allow hostname extraction from QUIC version 2 Initial packets.
Pick the payload RIPDPI should emit during UDP fake_burst steps when the original traffic is a QUIC Initial.
Only applies to the realistic preset. Leave it empty to use the built-in fake host inside the generated QUIC Initial.
Use a plain domain like video.example.org. IP literals and invalid hostnames are ignored.
The realistic preset will keep using the RIPDPI built-in fake host until you save a custom override.
The realistic preset will rewrite the generated QUIC Initial SNI to match this hostname.
QUIC routing and cache toggles above still control hostname extraction. This preset only changes the fake payload emitted by UDP fake_burst.
Keep the existing raw UDP fake payload path. Use this when you only want transport-level fake bursts.
Emit a fixed 620-byte compatibility blob that matches zapret-style QUIC fake payload behavior.
Build a valid IETF QUIC Initial, mirror v1 or v2 when possible, and optionally override the embedded host.
Visual QUIC fake payload settings are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --fake-quic-profile and --fake-quic-host in command line settings for the next start.
Enable UDP desync if you want RIPDPI to emit fake QUIC Initial packets during fake_burst steps.
RIPDPI will keep using the current raw UDP fake payload path even when fake_burst is configured.
Profile saved for UDP fake bursts
The QUIC fake profile is configured, but the current UDP chain has no fake_burst step yet. Add fake_burst in the chain editor to emit it.
Restart to apply QUIC fake payloads
The running RIPDPI session started before this QUIC fake payload change. Restart RIPDPI to apply the new profile.
RIPDPI will use the selected fake QUIC Initial profile on the next fake_burst that targets QUIC Initial traffic.
Applies only when the UDP chain includes fake_burst.
Applies to UDP fake_burst on QUIC Initial traffic only.
%1$d fake_burst packets configured by the UDP chain.
No fake_burst step is configured in the UDP chain yet.
Prefer raw RIPDPI flags over the visual editor.
Applied when command line settings are enabled.
Keep sampling connection telemetry, network state, and context while RIPDPI is running.
Enable runtime diagnostics history to edit sampling cadence.
How often RIPDPI stores network, telemetry, and context samples for an active connection. Allowed range: 5 to 300 seconds.
How long saved connection sessions, samples, diagnostics reports, and export records stay on-device before cleanup.
Attach stored history tables when building a diagnostics archive for sharing or local export.
Arguments below take precedence over the visual controls on this screen.
Check the highlighted fields before saving.
The editor is validating in real time. Fix the marked fields, then save again.
Enter a valid non-local DNS address.
Enter a valid proxy listen address.
Value is outside the allowed range.
Enter a valid strategy chain DSL.

[tcp] tlsrec extlen hostfake endhost+8 midhost=midsld host=googlevideo.com split midsld
[udp] fake_burst 3
Add a hostfake step when you want fake chunks only around the HTTP Host or TLS SNI span instead of faking the whole payload.
The saved strategy chain already contains hostfake steps and will use them on the next RIPDPI start.
Hostfake is configured in settings, but the running RIPDPI session started before this chain change.
Enable HTTP or HTTPS desync if you want hostfake to target Host or SNI fields.
Visual hostfake guidance stays visible here, but the active chain comes from raw RIPDPI arguments while command line settings are enabled.
Keep the real host as one span
Hostfake only touches Host/SNI-sized chunks. It does not use the full Fake TLS profile or fake offset marker.
Please stop the VPN service before changing settings
Set a specific resolver address for the local VPN path and keep it stored on this device.
DNS override is only applied while RIPDPI is running in local VPN mode.
Allow the local VPN builder to include IPv6 DNS routing when the selected network supports it.
Fast public resolver with a minimal setup footprint.
Widely reachable public resolver with familiar defaults.
Security-focused public resolver with threat blocking.
Public resolver that emphasizes ad and tracker blocking.
Review the latest local DNS, connection, warning, and error activity captured by RIPDPI on this device.
No local events have been captured yet.
Select one or more event types to narrow the stream.
Keep the latest matching entry in view while new diagnostics arrive.
Start RIPDPI or trigger a diagnostic event to populate the on-device stream.
No entries match the current filters
Adjust the type chips above to inspect a different part of the local buffer.
RIPDPI works locally. No remote servers, no accounts, no data leaves your phone.
Android will ask to create a local VPN. This routes traffic through the app on your device, and nothing is sent externally.
Start with a preset or configure manually. You can change everything later.
Tune how RIPDPI builds fake HTTPS ClientHello packets before they are split or sent.
Fake TLS settings are idle until the current strategy chain includes an HTTPS fake step.
Default uses RIPDPI’s built-in fake ClientHello. Original starts from the real outgoing ClientHello before applying fake-TLS mods.
Fixed keeps the configured fake SNI. Randomized generates a same-length hostname on each fake HTTPS packet.
Randomize the ClientHello random, Session ID, and KeyShare bytes in the fake packet.
Copy the Session ID from the real ClientHello when the lengths match.
Adjust TLS length fields so the real payload fits inside the fake packet’s padding accounting.
Controls the target fake TLS size before fake offset is resolved.
Visual fake TLS controls are paused while raw RIPDPI arguments are enabled. Use --fake-tls-mod, --fake-sni, and the rest of your command line profile for the next start.
The fake TLS profile is stored on this device, but RIPDPI will ignore it until HTTPS desync is enabled again.
A custom fake TLS profile is ready, but the current TCP chain does not include a fake step that can send a fake ClientHello.
Add a fake TCP step when you want RIPDPI to shape fake HTTPS ClientHello packets with the profile below.
RIPDPI will use the tuned fake ClientHello profile below whenever the current TCP chain emits fake HTTPS packets.
RIPDPI will use its default fake ClientHello with the profile below whenever the current TCP chain emits fake HTTPS packets.
Randomized hostname with the same length as the configured fake SNI
Randomize Random, Session ID, and KeyShare
Copy Session ID from the original ClientHello
Defined by the current raw RIPDPI command line profile.
Enable HTTPS desync before this profile can run.
Requires a fake TCP step in the strategy chain.
Used for HTTPS fake packets when the current visual strategy emits them.
Used for HTTPS fake packets after RIPDPI reconnects or restarts.
Resets the fake TLS profile to RIPDPI’s default ClientHello, fixed SNI, and input-sized envelope.
What does all of this mean?
Uncheck all to desync all traffic
Visual controls are unavailable while command line overrides are active.
Enable command line settings to edit raw RIPDPI flags here.
OOB data must be empty or a single character.
Android will ask to create a local VPN. This routes traffic through the app on your device — nothing is sent externally.
Grant Android VPN permission to keep RIPDPI running locally on this device.
RIPDPI uses a foreground service while the local VPN or proxy is active. Android requires a visible notification so the session stays transparent and you can quickly return to the running connection.
Notification permission is required before RIPDPI can start its local VPN or proxy.
Notification permission is still blocked. Open app settings and allow notifications for RIPDPI.
Allow RIPDPI to ignore battery optimizations so Android is less likely to interrupt the local VPN or proxy in the background.
Foreground service notifications are available for RIPDPI.
Required before Android can start the local VPN or proxy foreground service.
Required for the currently selected local VPN mode.
Only needed when you want to run RIPDPI in local VPN mode.
Grant Android VPN consent before RIPDPI can start the local VPN path.
Android is already allowing RIPDPI to run without battery optimization limits.
Recommended so the system is less likely to stop RIPDPI while it is running in the background.
Resolver, IPv6, and local tunnel DNS behavior.
Saved now and applied the next time VPN mode is active.
Low-level RIPDPI flags, proxy listeners, and protocol overrides.
Reduce peer-discovery leaks that can bypass the local tunnel.
Biometric unlock is enabled with a local backup PIN fallback.
Biometric unlock is enabled. Add a backup PIN for fallback access.
Ask for biometric authentication before opening the dashboard.
Set a four-digit fallback PIN so you can still unlock RIPDPI if biometrics are unavailable.
Store a four-digit PIN on this device for fallback authentication.
A four-digit fallback PIN is already stored locally.
Backup PIN must be exactly four digits.
Choose whether the app follows the system theme or stays fixed.
The interface updates immediately after you switch this preference.
App icon, adaptive shape, and themed icon options.
Changing the app icon may require your launcher to refresh. The icon update can take a few seconds to appear on your home screen.
Launcher-controlled mask used for this app icon on the current device.
Your launcher decides the final icon mask. RIPDPI can switch icon artwork, but the home app keeps control of the visible shape.
Different launchers can show circle, squircle, rounded-square, or custom icon masks.
If the icon artwork changes, your launcher may need a refresh before the new mask preview appears.
Use biometric authentication before opening the main dashboard on this device.
Only biometric authentication is configured for this device right now.
Use the fallback PIN stored on this device if biometric authentication is unavailable.
Digits only. The PIN is checked locally and never leaves this device.
The backup PIN does not match.
Compose-based local configuration surface for the preserved RIPDPI engine, service, and DataStore stack.
RIPDPI builds on the open-source hufrea/ripdpi project and keeps all routing and configuration local to this device.
